content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

October 23, 1995

Honorable Dennis Fisicaro
Mayor, Town of Colma
1198 El Camino Real
Colma, CA 94014

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-317

Dear Mr. Fisicaro:

This is in response to your request for advice as Mayor for
the City of Colma regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. How is the 12-month period set forth in Section 87103(c)
measured for disqualification purposes?

2. Are you disqualified from participating in decisions
affecting a former client at your November 8, 1995, town council
meeting where you have not received $250 from the client in the
past twelve months?

CONCLUSIONS

1. For purposes of Section 87103(c), you must count back
twelve months from the date of any decision. If you have not been
provided with, received, or been promised income aggregating $250
or more within the 12-month period preceding the decision, you
will not be precluded from participating in the decision by virtue
of subdivision (c) of Section 87103.

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-317
Page 2


     2.  No. You are not disqualified from participating in
decisions affecting a former client at your November 8, 1995, town
council meeting since the aggregate total income received from the
client during the preceding twelve months which is attributable to
you is less than $250.

                              FACTS

     You are the Mayor for the Town of Colma.  You also own a 50-
percent interest in a landscaping business.  One of your former
clients will have a matter pending before the town council at its
November 8, 1995, meeting.  The former client made payments to
your business in the amount of $100 per month.  As of October
1994, payments of $100 each were received from the client as
follows:  October 12, 1994, November 8, 1994, December 5, 1994,
January 3, 1995, and February 1, 1995.  As of February 1, 1995, no
other payments have been received by you from the former client.

                              ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 sets forth the general rule concerning
potential conflicts of interest confronting public officials.  It
states that no public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making, or in any way
attempt to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest.

     An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on:

          (a)  Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     $1,000 or more.

                              * * *

          (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
     and other than loans by a commercial lending
     institution in the regular course of business on
     terms available to the public without regard to
     official status, aggregating $250 or more in value
     provided to, received by or promised to the public
     official within 12 months prior to the time when
     the decision is made.

          (d)  Any business entity in which the public
     official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
     employee, or holds any position of management.

                              * * *

Our File No. A-95-317
Page 3


     For purposes of Section 87103, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

     Section 87103 (a), (c) and (d).

     As Mayor for the Town of Colma, you are a public official.
(Section 82048.) You have an interest in a business entity, your
landscaping business, within the meaning of Sections 87103(a) and
(d). In addition, since your ownership interest in the business
equals or exceeds 10-percent, clients of the landscaping business
are sources of income to you within the meaning of Section
87103(c).2 Accordingly, you must disqualify yourself from
participating in any decision which will have a reasonably
foreseeable3 material4 financial effect on the landscaping
business, or clients who are sources of income to you of $250 or
more, which is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.

     According to your facts, prior to February 1, 1995, your
landscaping business received payments of $100 per month from a
client who will appear before the town council on November 8,
1995. Therefore, you are disqualified from participating in
decisions concerning the former client if the total amount
received from that client by the business aggregates $500 within
the past 12 months, since your pro-rata share of the client's
income would be $250.

2 "Income" of an individual also includes a pro rata share of any
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).)

3 Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d
983, 989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; In re
Thorpe (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.198.)

4 Regulations 18702 - 18702.6 set forth the guidelines for
determining whether an official's economic interest in a decision
is "materially" affected as required by Section 87103.

Our File No. A-95-317
Page 4


To determine the date disqualification ends for purposes of
Section 87103, you must count back 12 months from the date of any
decision which you intend to make, participate in making, or use
your official position to influence. (Koppes Advice Letter, No.
I-90-517.) In this case, the "12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made" is the period beginning November 6, 1994,
and ending November 7, 1995. (Also see, Grunwald Advice Letter,
No. A-95-184.)

During the disqualification period, your business received
payments of $100 in November and December of 1994 and in January
and February of 1995 as shown below.5

Date Payments Received    Amount Received    Your Pro-Rata Share

   November 8, 1994           $100                $50
   December 5, 1994           $100                $50
   January 3, 1995            $100                $50
   February 1, 1995           $100                $50
   March 1995                 $  0                $ 0
   April 1995                 $  0                $ 0
   May 1995                   $  0                $ 0
   June 1995                  $  0                $ 0
   July 1995                  $  0                $ 0
   August 1995                $  0                $ 0
   September 1995             $  0                $ 0
   October 1995               $  0                $ 0
                              ____                ____

   Aggregate Total            $400                $200

     Since you own 50 percent of the business, your pro-rata share
of the business's payments each month during the disqualification
period totals $50 and aggregates less than $250. Therefore, since
you will not have received $250 during the twelve months preceding
the decision, you may participate in the town council decisions at
the council meeting of November 8, 1995.




_____________________________
5  The October 12, 1994, payment does not count for purposes of
determining the aggregate total received since it was received
outside the disqualification period.

Our File No. A-95-317
Page 5


If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at 916/322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
